summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft and Apple, while not direct competitors, are 'orthogonal rivals' whose fortunes have inversely tracked each other. In the mid-2000s, Microsoft suffers from a deep malaise around Windows Vista and a loss of corporate identity now that it lacks clear external enemies to defeat. Apple, meanwhile, is brimming with confidence — exemplified by the new MacBooks that offer flagship-level performance at consumer prices, showing Apple is pursuing market share over profit margins. Gruber contends that Microsoft's problems stem from middle management complexity and a culture that values bureaucratic decisions over design clarity, as evidenced by Vista's seven confusing editions versus Apple's simplified product line. He provocatively argues that a strong Vista would actually benefit Mac users by pressuring Apple to innovate.
key_points: Microsoft's greatest competitor is now its own older products — it must convince users to upgrade rather than defeat external rivals, which undermines the competitive identity that historically drove the company.
A weak Windows Vista is bad for Mac users because competitive pressure from Windows is what forces Apple to improve Mac OS X.

Apple's confidence is demonstrated by the new MacBooks offering near-MacBook-Pro performance at $1099, pursuing volume over per-unit profit margins — a reversal of the old iBook-era strategy of deliberately crippling cheaper machines.
Microsoft's plan to ship Vista in seven editions epitomizes a company that values management complexity over design clarity.
Microsoft's malaise is a management problem, not a talent problem — too many dependencies and too little accountability make it impossible to identify which teams are actually failing.
money_quotes: But it won't be good for Mac users if Vista turns out to be a dud. It might be good for Apple, but it won't be good for Mac users.
Microsoft, as a company, defines itself by its rivalries.
To sell new copies of Microsoft Office, they need to beat older copies of Microsoft Office.
Microsoft is suffering from a lack of confidence, and Apple is brimming with it.
Microsoft is turning into a company that values management decisions that increase complexity over design decisions that increase clarity.
This is how a company with so many talented programmers can spend six years developing an operating system that no one is excited about.
It is difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends upon his not understanding it.
key_insight: Microsoft's loss of external rivals has created an identity crisis and management bloat that produces complexity instead of innovation, while Apple's confidence is driving it toward simpler products, lower prices, and greater market share.
